[3.4. Data View	10](#3.4.-data-view)

# **1\. Design Constraints** {#1.-design-constraints}

* Scalability: The system must support up to 100 concurrent users in v1.0, with architecture designed to support horizontal scaling for v2.0 without code changes.  
* Performance: Study session start (due-card query) ≤ 500ms P95. Card review API response ≤ 500ms. Statistics forecast ≤ 200ms. Bulk JSON import (1000 cards) ≤ 5s.  
* Security: All passwords stored as bcrypt hashes (salt rounds ≥ 10). JWT-based stateless authentication (HS256). Account locked after 5 consecutive failed login attempts. Resource-Level Authorization enforces owner-only mutation of decks and cards.  
* Availability: Target uptime 99%. Docker restart:always policy. Offline study must continue without network; reviews queued locally in IndexedDB and synced on reconnect. Zero data loss on container restart.  
* Reliability: All mutations (card review, profile update, cascade delete) wrapped in atomic DB transactions with rollback on failure.  
* Modifiability: 4-layer architecture enables localized changes. Adding pagination to DeckRepository requires zero changes to DeckController. SM-2 algorithm is a swappable pure function.  
* Interoperability: JSON format for all import/export operations. REST API is platform-agnostic, supporting future mobile clients.  
* Deployment: Single-server v1.0 via Docker (Nginx \+ Node.js \+ PostgreSQL). Stateless Node.js design enables horizontal scaling in v2.0 with only Nginx upstream configuration changes.

# **2\. Quality Attribute Requirements** {#2.-quality-attribute-requirements}

## **2.1. Security** {#2.1.-security}

### **2.1.1. Authentication and Authorization** {#2.1.1.-authentication-and-authorization}

| Element | Statement |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Stimulus** | Users attempt to log in, register, reset passwords, or access protected endpoints. Malicious actors attempt brute-force login or cross-user resource mutations. |
| **Stimulus source** | Learners, Admins (authenticated); unauthenticated attackers. |
| **Environment** | Normal operation via Browser SPA (React) over HTTPS. JWT in HttpOnly, SameSite=Strict cookie on all protected routes. |
| **Artifact** | AuthController, AuthService, BruteForceGuard, AuthMiddleware, ResourceAuthMiddleware, login\_attempts table, JWT infrastructure. |
| **Response** | Password hashing using bcrypt (salt rounds ≥ 10\) at registration. JWT issued on successful login (HS256, HttpOnly cookie). AuthMiddleware verifies JWT signature and expiry on every protected request. BruteForceGuard tracks login\_attempts per (email, IP); locks account after 5 failures, auto-unlocks after timeout, fails-open on DB error (logged). ResourceAuthMiddleware enforces owner-only mutation: calls DeckRepo.findByIdAndOwner(deckId, userId); returns 403 Forbidden (MSG-AUTH-01) if null. DeckController is never invoked on authorization failure. Generic error message MSG03 ('Invalid credentials') prevents user enumeration. |
| **Response measure** | 100% passwords stored as bcrypt hashes (rounds ≥ 10). JWT verification completed per request. Account locked after exactly 5 consecutive failures. 100% of cross-user DELETE/PATCH attempts blocked with 403 ≤ 200ms. 0% of authorization failures expose business logic layer. |

### **2.1.2. Data Protection** {#2.1.2.-data-protection}

| Element | Statement |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Stimulus** | System stores, processes, or transmits sensitive user data including passwords, email addresses, and study history. |
| **Stimulus source** | Learners registering, logging in, updating profiles, or viewing statistics (UC-01, UC-04, UC-05). |
| **Environment** | Data lifecycle: browser input → HTTPS transmission → Node.js processing → PostgreSQL storage. All communication within Docker internal network for DB access. |
| **Artifact** | UserController, UserService, UserRepository, ProfileDTO, PostgreSQL users table, Nginx HTTPS termination. |
| **Response** | All client-server communication enforced over HTTPS (Nginx redirects HTTP to HTTPS). password\_hash field explicitly excluded from all API responses via ProfileDTO mapping in UserService — no ORM lazy-load leaks. DB queries always include userId in WHERE clause (data isolation tactic T3). PostgreSQL accessible only from Node.js containers via Docker internal network (port :5432 not exposed externally). bcrypt rounds ≥ 10 verified at registration, re-hashed only if password is modified on update. |
| **Response measure** | 0 API responses in any test scenario contain password\_hash, salt, or raw credentials. HTTPS enforced on 100% of client-server communication. PostgreSQL port :5432 not reachable from internet. bcrypt rounds ≥ 10 confirmed at registration (UC-01). |

### **2.1.3. Brute-Force Protection** {#2.1.3.-brute-force-protection}

| Element | Statement |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Stimulus** | A malicious actor submits repeated failed login attempts for a target account within a short time window. |
| **Stimulus source** | Unauthenticated external attacker with knowledge of a valid email address. |
| **Environment** | Normal operation; attacker submits ≥ 5 consecutive failed login attempts for the same email/IP combination. |
| **Artifact** | AuthController, BruteForceGuard, login\_attempts table in PostgreSQL. |
| **Response** | BruteForceGuard records each failed attempt in login\_attempts (email, IP, timestamp). After 5th consecutive failure: 429 Too Many Requests returned with MSG-LOCK-01; all subsequent attempts return 423 Locked until timeout expires. Auto-unlock occurs after lockout period without manual intervention. On DB error in BruteForceGuard: fail-open policy (login proceeds; error logged) prevents availability degradation. Error message MSG03 never distinguishes wrong password from wrong email. |
| **Response measure** | Account locked after exactly 5 consecutive failures. Lockout response delivered ≤ 200ms. 0% false-positive lockout for legitimate users. Fail-open on DB error confirmed by logging — no availability loss. |

## **2.2. Performance** {#2.2.-performance}

### **2.2.1. Study Session Latency** {#2.2.1.-study-session-latency}

| Element | Statement |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Stimulus** | Learner clicks 'Study Now' on a deck. System must execute findDueCards(deckId, userId, today) and return the first card for display. |
| **Stimulus source** | Authenticated Learner during normal study operation (UC-20). |
| **Environment** | Normal operation; up to 100 concurrent users; target deck has up to 500 cards; total DB contains up to 50,000 card records. |
| **Artifact** | StudyController, CardRepository.findDueCards(), PostgreSQL composite index on cards(deckId, userId, nextReviewDate). |
| **Response** | Composite DB index (T1) enables indexed SQL query: WHERE deckId=? AND userId=? AND nextReviewDate \<= today ORDER BY nextReviewDate ASC LIMIT N. Results sorted overdue-first, then new cards. SM2Service.calculate() executes as a pure in-process function (\< 1ms, zero I/O) — no async overhead. Index scan confirmed via EXPLAIN ANALYZE (no sequential scan). First card returned to SPA for rendering. |
| **Response measure** | SQL query execution ≤ 300ms. Full API response (StudyController → SPA) ≤ 500ms P95. EXPLAIN ANALYZE confirms index scan, not sequential scan, on 50k-record dataset. |

### **2.2.2. Deck Library Pagination** {#2.2.2.-deck-library-pagination}

| Element | Statement |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Stimulus** | Learner with 100+ decks navigates to Dashboard. System must load the Deck Library without full-table retrieval. |
| **Stimulus source** | Authenticated Learner (UC-07) during normal operation. |
| **Environment** | Normal operation; user has 150 deck records; 100 concurrent users on system. |
| **Artifact** | DeckController, DeckService, DeckRepository.findByUserIdPaginated(), PostgreSQL index on decks(userId, updated\_at). |
| **Response** | DeckRepository.findByUserIdPaginated(userId, page, size=20) executes: SELECT … FROM decks WHERE user\_id=? ORDER BY updated\_at DESC LIMIT 20 OFFSET ?. DB index on (userId, updated\_at) eliminates sequential scan. Returns {decks: Deck\[\], total: int, page: int}. SPA renders page 1 with pagination controls showing total count. DeckController API contract unchanged when pagination was added to DeckRepository — zero controller changes required. |
| **Response measure** | First page renders ≤ 1 second. DB query ≤ 300ms. Index on (userId, updated\_at) confirmed active. Pagination addition required zero changes to DeckController (Low Coupling verified). |

### **2.2.3. Bulk Import Throughput** {#2.2.3.-bulk-import-throughput}

| Element | Statement |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Stimulus** | Learner uploads a JSON file containing 1000 vocabulary cards for import into a deck (UC-18). |
| **Stimulus source** | Authenticated Learner performing batch data import. |
| **Environment** | Normal operation; single import request; 1000-card JSON payload. |
| **Artifact** | ImportExportService, CardRepository.batchInsert(), PostgreSQL cards table. |
| **Response** | ImportExportService validates each card against JSON schema; invalid items are skipped (not thrown). Valid cards passed to CardRepository.batchInsert() as a single bulk INSERT statement. Skip-invalid strategy ensures partial success: {created: N, skipped: M} returned. No per-card round-trip to DB. |
| **Response measure** | 1000-card import completes ≤ 5 seconds. Invalid items skipped individually without aborting the batch. Result {created, skipped} returned to client. |

## **2.3. Reliability** {#2.3.-reliability}

### **2.3.1. Atomic Transaction Integrity** {#2.3.1.-atomic-transaction-integrity}

| Element | Statement |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Stimulus** | User submits a card review rating. Mid-transaction DB connection failure occurs after card.update() executes but before review.create() completes. |
| **Stimulus source** | Infrastructure failure — DB connection pool exhaustion or network interruption (UC-21, AD-04). |
| **Environment** | Degraded: DB connection pool at maximum; connection lost mid-transaction. |
| **Artifact** | ReviewService, PostgreSQL transaction (BEGIN → CardRepo.update → ReviewRepo.create → COMMIT), global Express error handler. |
| **Response** | PostgreSQL rolls back the entire transaction on connection failure. Card state reverts to pre-update values. Review history record is not created. No partial state persists. Express global error handler (app.use((err,req,res,next) \=\> ...)) catches the DB error and returns 503 Service Unavailable to the client. Consistent state is preserved — card and review are always written together or neither. |
| **Response measure** | Zero partial states persisted across 100% of transaction failures. Card and review always written atomically (both succeed or both roll back). 503 delivered to client within 2 seconds of failure. Verified by: intentional connection kill mid-transaction in integration test. |

### **2.3.2. Cascade Delete Integrity** {#2.3.2.-cascade-delete-integrity}

| Element | Statement |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Stimulus** | User deletes a deck containing cards and associated review history (UC-10), or deletes their account (UC-06). |
| **Stimulus source** | Authenticated Learner confirming destructive action via UI confirmation dialog. |
| **Environment** | Normal operation; deck contains N cards each with M review records. |
| **Artifact** | DeckController / UserController, DeckRepository, CardRepository, ReviewRepository, PostgreSQL FK constraints with ON DELETE CASCADE. |
| **Response** | Application-level transaction initiates cascade: reviews → cards → deck (or for account: reviews → cards → decks → user). FK ON DELETE CASCADE provides a second safety layer in the DB schema. Full rollback on any step failure. Confirmation dialog in SPA prevents accidental deletion. |
| **Response measure** | Zero orphan records after any delete operation. All cascade deletes complete atomically — all-or-nothing. FK constraints verified to be in place on schema inspection. |

## **2.4. Availability** {#2.4.-availability}

### **2.4.1. Offline Study Session** {#2.4.1.-offline-study-session}

| Element | Statement |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Stimulus** | Learner is mid-session (10 of 30 cards reviewed) when network connection drops. User continues reviewing remaining 20 cards without connectivity. |
| **Stimulus source** | Learner on mobile or unreliable network (e.g., subway, rural area) — UC-21, AD-02 (highest Technical Risk: H). |
| **Environment** | Degraded: navigator.onLine \= false; IndexedDB available in browser; server unreachable. |
| **Artifact** | OfflineCacheUI (SPA), IndexedDB (offline\_queue), OfflineSyncService, POST /api/study/review/batch endpoint. |
| **Response** | SPA detects offline state via window 'offline' event; displays offline banner. OfflineSyncService.queueReview({cardId, rating, timestamp}) stores each review in IndexedDB — asynchronous, transaction-safe, survives browser tab reload. Session continues from locally cached card queue with zero server calls. On window 'online' event: OfflineSyncService.syncPending() retrieves all queued reviews from IndexedDB and sends POST /api/study/review/batch \+ JWT. Server processes each review atomically. IndexedDB cleared on 200 OK {synced: N, failed: 0}. |
| **Response measure** | 0 review outcomes lost during network outage. Zero additional latency for offline card submissions (local IndexedDB only). Batch sync completes ≤ 5 seconds for 30 queued reviews on reconnection. IndexedDB chosen over localStorage: async, transaction-safe, larger capacity, no serialization overhead. |

### **2.4.2. Container Fault Recovery** {#2.4.2.-container-fault-recovery}

| Element | Statement |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Stimulus** | Node.js application container crashes due to unhandled exception or OOM, or PostgreSQL container restarts. |
| **Stimulus source** | Software bug, resource exhaustion, infrastructure event. |
| **Environment** | Production Docker deployment; active users may be mid-session. |
| **Artifact** | Docker Compose configuration (restart:always), PostgreSQL persistent volume, Express global error handler. |
| **Response** | Docker restart:always policy automatically restarts failed container within \~30 seconds. PostgreSQL persistent volume (EBS / cloud disk) ensures all committed data survives container restart — zero data loss for committed transactions. Express global error handler prevents unhandled promise rejections from crashing the Node.js process on non-fatal errors. StatisticsService failure degrades gracefully — returns empty/cached stats without affecting the study session. |
| **Response measure** | Container restart completes within 30 seconds. Zero data loss for committed DB transactions on restart (persistent volume verified). System uptime target: 99%. StatisticsService failure does not degrade study session availability. |

## **2.5. Scalability** {#2.5.-scalability}

### **2.5.1. Horizontal Scaling for Concurrent Users** {#2.5.1.-horizontal-scaling-for-concurrent-users}

| Element | Statement |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Stimulus** | User load exceeds single-server capacity; CPU \> 70% or RAM \> 80% threshold crossed. |
| **Stimulus source** | Growing learner base; increased concurrent study sessions. |
| **Environment** | v2.0 production deployment; Docker Host with Nginx load balancer upstream. |
| **Artifact** | Nginx load balancer, Node.js App instances (\#1…\#N), PostgreSQL Primary \+ Read Replica, Docker orchestration. |
| **Response** | Stateless Node.js design (JWT carries all auth state — no server-side session) enables direct horizontal scaling: add Node.js instance to Nginx upstream block, zero code changes required. Nginx round-robin load balancing with health-check (GET /api/health → 200 OK). Each Node.js instance maintains its own connection pool (max 10 connections to PostgreSQL). StatisticsService aggregation queries routed to PostgreSQL Read Replica to reduce write-path latency. Scaling trigger: CPU \> 70% → add Node.js instance; RAM \> 80% → scale DB. |
| **Response measure** | New Node.js instance added with zero application code changes (Nginx upstream config only). No session affinity required — any instance handles any request. Connection pool per instance: max 10\. Read replica absorbs statistics query load. |

## **2.6. Modifiability** {#2.6.-modifiability}

### **2.6.1. Algorithm and Business Logic Changes** {#2.6.1.-algorithm-and-business-logic-changes}

| Element | Statement |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Stimulus** | SM-2 spaced-repetition algorithm needs to be upgraded (e.g., to SM-5 or FSRS), or a business rule in deck/card management changes. |
| **Stimulus source** | Product team or academic requirement update. |
| **Environment** | Development or maintenance phase; system live in production. |
| **Artifact** | SM2Service (pure function), ReviewService, DeckRepository, DeckController. |
| **Response** | SM2Service is a pure function (zero I/O, zero external dependencies) — algorithm upgrade is a single-class swap with no impact on ReviewService or any repository. Low Coupling is enforced by the Module Dependency Diagram: all dependencies flow downward only; no controller calls a Repository directly. Adding pagination to DeckRepository required zero changes to DeckController — the interface contract is unchanged. Repository interfaces allow swapping PostgreSQL implementation without altering business layer. |
| **Response measure** | SM-2 → SM-5 replacement requires changes to SM2Service only (zero cascade to ReviewService, CardRepository, or controllers). Pagination addition to DeckRepository confirmed zero DeckController changes. No upward dependencies exist in Module Dependency Diagram. |
